title: Alcaraz Survives Medvedev Five-Set Thriller to Reach Semi-Finals
subtitle: The Spaniard dug deep in a pulsating encounter lasting three hours and forty-two minutes on Centre Court
excerpt: Carlos Alcaraz produced some of the most extraordinary tennis of his career as he recovered from a set down to defeat Daniil Medvedev in a captivating quarter-final.
body:
Carlos Alcaraz produced some of the most extraordinary tennis of his career on Tuesday afternoon, recovering from a set down to defeat Daniil Medvedev 4-6, 7-5, 6-3, 4-6, 7-5 in a captivating quarter-final that left the Centre Court crowd breathless.

The world number three showed his champion's temperament when it mattered most, saving three match points in the fourth set before finding another gear in the decider to advance to his third successive Wimbledon semi-final.

"I knew it was going to be incredibly tough today," Alcaraz said in his on-court interview. "Daniil is one of the best players in the world on any surface, and on grass he has improved so much. I'm just really happy with how I fought."

Medvedev, who appeared to have the match in hand at 5-3 up in the fifth, unravelled in the most dramatic fashion as Alcaraz reeled off four consecutive games to seal victory.
imageAlt: Carlos Alcaraz celebrating on Centre Court

title: How Djokovic Dismantled Rune's Game Plan in Four Sets
subtitle: A tactical breakdown of the chess match that unfolded on No. 1 Court
excerpt: Novak Djokovic's victory over Holger Rune was as much a triumph of tactical intelligence as it was of physical excellence. We break down the key adjustments that proved decisive.
body:
There was a moment in the third set, with Holger Rune leading 3-1, when it looked as though the Dane had finally cracked the Djokovic code. The return game was working. The forehand was landing deep. The crowd sensed an upset.

Then Djokovic did what Djokovic does. He adjusted. Quietly, methodically, without fuss.
imageAlt: Novak Djokovic in action on No. 1 Court

title: Sinner's Clinical Display Against Paul Signals Semi-Final Intent
subtitle: World number one drops just nine games as he marches into the last four
excerpt: Jannik Sinner was utterly imperious in his 6-3, 6-4, 6-2 destruction of Tommy Paul, a performance that underlined why the Italian is the heavy favourite to claim his first Wimbledon title.
body: If there were any doubts about Jannik Sinner's readiness to challenge for the Wimbledon title, the world number one emphatically dispelled them with a masterful performance against Tommy Paul.
imageAlt: Jannik Sinner serving on No. 2 Court

title: Rybakina Outlasts Gauff in Absorbing Three-Set Battle
subtitle: The Kazakh's booming serve proved the difference in a high-quality contest on No. 1 Court
excerpt: Elena Rybakina's formidable serving and sharp volleying were enough to see off the spirited challenge of Coco Gauff, ending the American's hopes of a first Wimbledon title.
body: Elena Rybakina booked her place in the Wimbledon semi-finals with a 6-4, 6-7(4), 6-3 victory over Coco Gauff in a match that ebbed and flowed beautifully before the Kazakh's superior grass-court credentials proved decisive.

title: Order of Play: Day Nine — All You Need to Know
subtitle: Everything scheduled for Wednesday at The All England Club
excerpt: Wednesday brings the conclusion of the quarter-finals, with Sabalenka, Keys, Zverev and Shelton all set to contest spots in the last four.
body: Wednesday's order of play promises another feast of high-quality tennis at The All England Club, with the final two quarter-finals of the Championships completing the semi-final line-up.

title: Strawberries, White and Silence: The Traditions That Define Wimbledon
subtitle: From the dress code to the royal box, we explore the customs that make The Championships unlike any other event in sport
excerpt: No sporting event clings to its traditions quite like Wimbledon. But where do they come from, and why do they endure in an era of constant change?
body: Every June, as the grass courts of SW19 are prepared for another Championships, something remarkable happens. The world's greatest tennis players submit to a set of rules that have remained largely unchanged for more than a century.
